data.title: Spotify: I See The Stock As A Top Hit Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
In my view, Spotify (SPOT) is the go-to music streaming service, and I believe the "go-to" moat is what makes the company attractive at current levels.

The company will be releasing its Q1 towards the end of April, and I will be looking for an encore performance to its Q4 release.

When that was released in early February, the company reported strong user growth and better-than-expected earnings. This helped send shares up about 15% following the release.

With the stock trading at the lower half of its 52-week range and still down about 10% YTD, I believe investors are afforded a reasonable entry point in a market leader, which I believe will remain dominant in the months and years ahead.

SPOT Stock Key Metrics

SPOT isn’t a discounted stock by any means. Shares are graded poorly on valuation by Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quant scores, and the multiples do rightly seem elevated.

Its current forward earnings multiple, for example, stands at over 30x. In addition, its enterprise value hovers in the mid-20x to mid-30x range of both EBITDA and EBIT. Bargain hunters are likely not seeing basement bin pricing here.

However, I believe the premium pricing is very much warranted. The company is reporting double-digit percentage sales growth and is crushing its earnings estimates.

In its Q4 release, for example, SPOT beat its earnings estimates by a whopping €1.65/share. I also believe SPOT faces very little competition from other streaming alternatives, such as Apple (AAPL) Music and Amazon’s (AMZN) streaming service.

Sentiment across the analyst community is similarly bullish, with both the SA community and Wall Street highly bullish on SPOT’s prospects. And among Wall Street, consensus targets see over 20% upside potential remaining in the stock.

I very much believe shares can reach this target, especially with continued user growth and earnings strength.

What Is The Outlook For SPOT Stock?

When drilling into the Q4 transcript, I circled the excerpt where founder Daniel Ek framed SPOT as a company that wins at the intersection of consumers and creators.

To me, that’s an important statement to unpack. Here’s the way I see it. Features like Discover Weekly, Wrapped, Spotify for Artists, and the expanding free tier aren’t just product tweaks. They are, instead, proof that SPOT continues to deepen the flywheel between listeners, artists, and advertisers. That ecosystem is only getting stronger, in my view.

What also stood out to me is how aggressively SPOT is leaning into AI and personalization. The team appears to clearly see SPOT as a technology platform for audio, not just a music app.

Tools like Interactive DJ, Prompted Playlists, and advanced mixing features appear to be driving more engagement, more time spent, and ultimately higher lifetime value per user. I see that as a powerful setup for both subscriber growth and monetization.

Financially, I believe the story is lining up with the product vision. Revenue grew 13% in Q4, operating income soared more than 50% for the full year, and free cash flow hit a record €2.9 billion.

With pricing increases sticking, churn staying low, and margins expected to expand further in 2026, I believe that SPOT looks positioned for continued earnings strength alongside its user growth story.

What To Watch When SPOT Reports Q1 Results?

When SPOT reports Q1, I’ll be watching the user numbers first, because that’s ultimately the core of everything that SPOT does.

During Q4, SPOT guided to 759M MAUs and 293 million subscribers. This implies 8 million MAU net adds and 3 million subscriber net adds in what is seasonally the slowest quarter of the year.

In my view, if SPOT can meet or exceed these targets, it would support the idea that user growth here is durable, even after recent price increases.

I’ll also be paying very close attention to ARPU, which is expected to grow 5%–6%. That’s important because it would show that pricing power is sticking without driving meaningful churn. SPOT already noted that churn has behaved in line with expectations, which is exactly what you, as a business, would want to hear after raising prices.

Margins are another key watch item. Q1 gross margin is guided at 32.8%, and the company expects margins to improve through 2026 as revenue growth outpaces content costs. If we see early signs of that playing out, it strengthens the earnings growth narrative, in my view.

Finally, any commentary around AI-driven features like Interactive DJ and Prompted Playlists, and how they are impacting engagement and retention, will be crucial.

Is SPOT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my view, SPOT is a "Buy" ahead of Q1.

Yes, the valuation looks rich on the surface. A forward earnings multiple north of 30x and EV multiples in the 20x–30x range are not going to attract deep value investors. But I don’t think SPOT should be looked at through a bargain-bin lens.

In my view, this is a company that is growing users at scale, expanding margins, generating record free cash flow, and increasingly proving it has real pricing power.

More importantly, I believe SPOT’s moat is widening. It’s not just the largest music streaming platform. In my view, it’s becoming the default platform where audio content is discovered, shared, and monetized.

Furthermore, the ecosystem between listeners, creators, and advertisers appears to be getting tighter, and the company’s aggressive push into AI-driven personalization is making the product even stickier, in my view.

When you pair that with double-digit revenue growth, operating income up more than 50% last year, and expectations for further margin expansion in 2026, the premium valuation starts to make a lot more sense.

With the stock still trading near the lower half of its 52-week range and consensus targets pointing to roughly 20% upside, I see this as an attractive entry point into a category leader that I believe still has plenty of runway ahead.

data.title: With Warsh Confirmation Pending Focus Reverts To Shrinking Fed Balance Sheet
data.description:
Warsh Confirmation Pending

Fed Chair Nominee Kevin Warsh is supposed to assume his new position when current Fed Chair Jay Powell’s term expires on May 15th, one month from today.

There’s a small matter that needs to take place first, though, and that is Warsh is required to be confirmed by the full Senate.

There are two parts to the confirmation. First, Warsh must be confirmed for a seat on the Board of Governors, which comes with a 14-year term, and separately, he must be confirmed as Chair, which is for a 4-year period.

Procedurally, Warsh must file the necessary Office of Government Ethics paperwork with the Senate Banking Committee, clearing the way for a confirmation hearing before the Committee. These documents include a financial disclosure form and an ethics agreement.

That step was completed on Monday.

With that box checked, the hearing date was set for next Tuesday, April 21st.

The hearing is public, where Senators will question the nominee on critical issues such as his qualifications and his views on interest rates, inflation, the role of the Fed, and Fed independence.

Warsh’s Views On The Fed

We have a precursor to Warsh’s thinking from an Opinion Article he published in the Wall Street Journal in November, while he was in the middle of the interview process for the Fed Chair position.

In “The Federal Reserve’s Broken Leadership” Warsh highlighted four key points:

  1. AI will be a significant disinflationary force in the coming years.
  2. The Fed’s bloated balance sheet can be reduced significantly.
  3. The Fed’s regulatory failures led to the banking crisis of 2023.
  4. The Fed has wasted 10 years trying to work on global bank regulations, known as the Basel Accords, while they should be focusing on what works for the U.S. banking industry and letting the world follow us.

It is clear Warsh wants to take the Fed in a new direction.

The Fed’s Bloated Balance Sheet

Of the issues Warsh highlighted, the Fed’s bloated balance sheet has gotten the most attention recently.

This is a topic Warsh is quite familiar with, as he was previously a Fed Governor from 2006-2011, during which time we experienced the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) of 2008. The Fed was very aggressive in their efforts to prevent the economy from collapsing. They initially used their most effective monetary policy tool by cutting the fed funds rate to almost zero. Once that tool was no longer available to them, they turned to more unconventional methods.

Using Open Market Operations, the Fed embarked on a series of Large-Scale Asset Purchases, more commonly known as Quantitative Easing (QE), to try to stimulate the economy and add liquidity to the system. This resulted in a massive expansion of their balance sheet.

QE was only intended as a stopgap measure, and once the economy stabilized, it was to be unwound. In 2011, after the crisis had passed, Warsh chose to resign from the Fed because he opposed the continuation of QE. Warsh emphasized that relying on low-interest rates risked inflating asset prices and encouraged leverage. He felt QE “involves the misallocations of capital in the economy and the misallocation of responsibility in our government.”

He believed efforts to strengthen the economy should be conducted through fiscal policy, not monetary policy.

The Fed balance sheet totaled $800 billion when Warsh first joined the Fed and grew to $2.5 trillion when he resigned.

As can be seen in the chart above, the Fed continued with QE, then initiated QE again during the pandemic. Altogether, the Fed implemented QE over a 14-year period, and the balance sheet peaked at almost $9 trillion.

Balance Sheet Reduction

The Fed finally implemented their Policy Normalization plan in 2022 when they began raising the fed funds rate and started reducing their balance sheet through maturity runoffs, more commonly known as Quantitative Tightening (QT.)

For 3 ½ years the Fed executed QT, shrinking the balance sheet by $2.4 trillion, a 27% decline. Then, in December 2025, the Fed determined that they had reached their desired level of ample reserves, and they ended QT.

Nominee Warsh thinks there is more room to go.

Research Supporting Balance Sheet Reduction

Within the past month there have been three major studies explaining how the Fed can reduce their balance sheet.

The first is “The Payment System Puts a Floor on the Fed’s Balance Sheet” by Darrell Duffie, a Professor of Finance at Stanford Business School.

The next is “A User’s Guide to Reducing the Federal Reserve’s Balance Sheet” by Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran.

And the final paper is “Options for reducing the size of the Fed’s balance sheet” by Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan.

There are many similarities among the papers. Each focuses on the liability side of the balance sheet, and explains ways to get banks to reduce their demand for bank reserves,

These methods include some regulatory changes, a tiering of the interest rates the Fed pays on reserves, and making the Fed’s liquidity tools more accessible, so banks don’t need to hold liquidity continuously.

Looking at the liability side makes sense because the Fed expanded their balance sheet to add liquidity to the financial system by creating bank reserves, so shrinking the balance sheet requires reducing the bank reserves.

However, I see a fundamental problem in ignoring the asset side of the balance sheet.

How To Deal With The Fed’s Unrealized Losses

Liquidity in the form of bank reserves is what the Fed created during QE, and liquidity is what the Fed sought to drain by reducing reserves under QT.

But the mechanism the Fed employed to create and drain reserves is through trading assets, or in this case securities held in the Fed’s System Open Market Account (SOMA.) The Fed bought U.S Treasury securities and Mortgage-Backed Securities during QE, and they comprise 99% of the Fed’s total assets.

In order to reduce their liabilities, the Fed must also reduce their assets, and this is where the problem creeps up.

During QE the Fed deviated from their historical practice of owning only short-term Treasury securities, and they bought longer maturity Treasuries and MBS. Most of these SOMA holdings were purchased when interest rates were much lower. As a result, their current market value is significantly below their cost.

In footnote 5 d in the Fed’s 2025 Combined Financial Statements they explain they are carrying a $535.3 billion unrealized loss in their Treasury holdings and a $309 billion unrealized loss on their MBS holdings. The total unrealized loss of $844 billion represents a 13% haircut from the purchase price of the securities.

The quickest way to shrink the balance sheet is to sell securities, but to do so would require the Fed to record the loss on the holdings.

That is something they have refused to do to date, and are unlikely to do in the future. The Fed has already dealt with large operating losses over the past three years, which cumulatively total $243 billion and are carried in a Deferred Asset account on the balance sheet.

Consequently, the only way to reduce their assets is through security runoffs, which is how Fed has handled QT so far.

This is an important question for the Senate Banking Committee to ask Warsh, how he plans to deal with SOMA losses if the Fed shrinks their balance sheet.

The Fed has consistently stated that their realized and unrealized losses do not affect monetary policy implementation or effectiveness.

But this is not the complete story.

In the Miran study, he addresses Fed losses. In fact, he explains that they are a reason for wanting to reduce the Fed balance sheet. He states “while a central bank can, in theory, operate with an infinitely negative capital position, those losses may impugn the Fed’s credibility and competence in the public mind and thereby threaten independence and scope for effective action. They also induce volatility in the remittance of operating profits to the Treasury since these profits are delayed until the Fed recoups the losses. Volatility in profit remittance induces additional volatility in fiscal deficits, which has its own set of consequences.”

Miran’s study is the first time a Fed official has suggested that Fed losses could be a problem and they validate the criticisms I have written about for more than three years in highlighting this Fed deficiency.

If, in fact, Warsh chooses to reduce the Fed’s balance sheet, it will be a slow process.

Balance Sheet Reduction is Tightening Policy

It should be remembered that shrinking the Fed’s balance sheet by draining reserves is a tightening of monetary policy. It is called Quantitative Tightening for a reason.

Warsh is also going to have to square this during his hearing with his purported aim of reducing rates. President Trump has stated many times that his litmus test for a Fed Chair would be someone who agreed with him about lowering rates. He said “If I think somebody’s going to keep rates where they are…I’m not going to put them in.”

Warsh will have to explain how he can reduce rates, which is an easing of policy, at the same time he is shrinking the balance sheet, which is a tightening of policy. It is like driving with one foot on the gas and one foot on the brake.

Potential Obstacles

Following the hearing, the Committee then votes on whether or not to move the nominee for a vote before the full Senate.

This is where things get tricky.

The Senate Banking Committee is comprised of 13 Republicans and 11 Democrats. A simple majority moves the nomination forward. Presently, Senator Thom Tillis, R-NC, has vowed to block any nominee until the Department of Justice investigation into current Chair Powell is closed.

Tillis has publicly stated “If there were any remaining doubt whether advisers within the Trump Administration were actively pushing to end the independence of the Federal Reserve, there should now be none. It is now the independence and credibility of the Department of Justice that are in question. I will oppose the confirmation of any nominee for the Fed – including the upcoming Fed Chair vacancy – until this legal matter is resolved.”

Despite U.S. District Judge James Boasberg deciding, twice, to block subpoenas issued by the DOJ for documents from the Federal Reserve and Chair Powell, citing there was no evidence of a crime, seemingly ending their criminal investigation, U.S Attorney Jeanine Pirro has indicated that the inquiry will continue.

Senator Tim Scott, who announced the that the hearing date has been set, stated that he thought the DOJ would finish up its investigation within weeks, which would allow Senator Tillis to vote in favor of Warsh. Scott did acknowledge, however, that he has no inside information on DOJ’s plan.

Senator Tillis has reaffirmed that he will not vote for any Fed nominee until the DOJ case against the Chair Powell has ended.

To complicate the confirmation process more, President Trump today said the investigation into the Fed renovation will continue.

All of these obstacles are pushing up against the clock to get Warsh confirmed. From Tuesday’s hearing, there are only 3 ½ weeks until May 15th for the Senate to confirm Warsh. The Senate is out of session for one of those weeks.

Something will have to give in order for Warsh to get the approval of the Banking Committee to be up for approval in front of the entire Senate.

If it does not happen by May 15th, Powell has stated that he will continue to serve as Chair pro tem until confirmation.

Stay tuned.

data.title: The Davenport Funds Q1 2026 Stock Market Update
data.description:
Q1 2026 Stock Market Update

Market Returns (%) Q1 2026 2025
U.S. Large Caps -4.33 17.88
U.S. Mid Caps 1.29 10.60
U.S. Small Caps 0.89 12.81
International Developed Markets -1.24 31.22
Emerging Markets -0.17 33.57
Intermediate Term Bonds -0.02 6.97

Source: Morningstar Direct. Please see last page for index definitions.

Topsy-Turvy

It's hard to write a letter these days. Any attempt to prepare thoughts in advance risks quickly being obsolesced (we hope this letter is still relevant when you read it!). Topsy-turvy is the best way to describe the first quarter of 2026. The year started on a decent note with gains in January. However, renewed tariff concerns, waning enthusiasm for AI-related stocks and war in Iran ultimately became headwinds. The S&P 500 ® Index finished the quarter down 4.33% while the Russell 2000 ® Index finished up 0.89% (having been up 9.54% at one point).

We initially witnessed a rotation within the market. Technology stocks and names linked to artificial intelligence (AI), which were so popular for much of last year, began to see declines. In fact, technology was one of the worst performing sectors this quarter (down 9%). Two potential risks that we discussed at year end started to manifest themselves. Number one was the potential for AI to fuel disruption among the large technology incumbents. This was on full display in Q1, with the various AI participants forming a circular firing squad of sorts. Many prior “winners” became “losers” for fear of disruption and/or loss of leadership status. This disruption narrative was most acute in the software space. The second risk we previously highlighted was the potential for investors to question the massive capital expenditures going into computing power for AI. Indeed, investors started to wince as tech behemoths dramatically increased spending plans while having uncertain paybacks.

Meanwhile, investors started to look for value elsewhere. Cyclical, value-oriented and small cap stocks broadly outperformed for a bit. Interestingly, the equal-weighted S&P 500 meaningfully outperformed the cap-weighted index, which is dominated by large cap tech bellweathers including the so-called “Magnificent 7” 1 . As the tech sector was declining, sectors such as energy, materials and industrials started the year on a strong note as investors began to discount an improving economic environment alongside fiscal stimulus and the potential for further interest rate cuts. We also think there was an element of investors gravitating towards businesses that were deemed more immune from the AI-disruption narrative. Ironically, it started to pay to be outside of the AI fray despite the promise of the new technology.

Then, conflict in Iran took over the headlines. The Strait of Hormuz effectively closed (20-25% of seaborne oil passes through the Strait), oil prices spiked and market dynamics changed as investors began to fear an economic slowdown. While energy stocks gained due to higher commodity prices, we shifted from a market rotation to a market where almost everything was going down. Technology continued to struggle, but consumer, cyclical and interest rate-sensitive stocks were also hit hard. The economic risks of war in Iran are fairly straightforward. For one, higher oil prices mean higher gasoline prices (up $0.75-$1.00 per gallon since the war commenced), which could weigh on consumers' wallets. Higher oil also means upward pressure on overall inflation, which in turn puts upward pressure on interest rates and financing costs. Relatedly, it also decreases the odds of monetary stimulus in the form of Fed rate cuts. Finally, many companies that have a presence in the Middle East are dealing with disruption to their businesses and/or supply chains.

Where now? We were looking forward to the potential benefits of both fiscal and monetary stimulus. The waters are now murkier and higher gasoline prices, should they persist, will offset much of the benefit of Trump's Big Beautiful Bill. However, the President seems very sensitive to both economic conditions and markets, especially with mid-term elections approaching. We aren't sure how easily this conflict can be resolved and the Middle East has always been a quagmire, but any semblance of a resolution, including re-opening the Strait, could prompt a relief rally (we got a taste of this on the last day of the quarter). Remember, we were thrown a Trump curveball this time last year in the form of Liberation Day and tariffs. Uncertainty initially weighed on investor sentiment, but it ultimately paid to lean into fear and be buyers of stocks as worst case outcomes never came to fruition.

Speaking of fear, the CNN Fear & Greed Index resides at 14 out of 100 as of this writing, indicating "extreme fear" on the part of investors. What's more, valuations have become more reasonable with the S&P 500 trading at 19.3x forward earnings and the equal-weighted S&P at 15.6x. Assuming a reasonably swift resolution to Iran (or at least a going from a boil to a simmer), we think many stocks now offer compelling deals. A volatile political environment has brought many surprises and made our job a little more difficult, but we do indeed see value in certain areas. We continue to focus on avoiding disruption at the hands of AI and remain hopeful that economic improvement can continue, thereby benefiting cyclical and consumer-oriented businesses. Thank you for your partnership and please refer to our portfolio letters for a discussion of themes and ideas.

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®

Chairman, Investment Policy Committee

1 The Magnificent Seven (“Mag 7”), stocks are a group of high-performing and influential companies in the U.S. stock market: Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla

data.title: MeiraGTx: Turning Dry Mouth Into Liquid Gold
data.description:
MeiraGTx Overview

With MeiraGTx’s (MGTX) stock up 50% in the past month, I wanted to see what the "fuss" is all about. Although this is a name I am familiar with, I have yet to do a formal evaluation.

MeiraGTx describes themselves as a “clinical-stage genetic medicine company committed to harnessing the full potential of genetic therapies for both rare and prevalent diseases.”

MeiraGTx does things a bit differently than, say, other gene therapy companies, like CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP). MeiraGTx uses riboswitch gene regulation to allow for precise, dose-responsive control of gene expression using an oral small-molecule inducer.

MeiraGTx is also unique in that it is “vertically integrated.” That is, it does not rely on contract manufacturing organizations (like many of its peers in biotech).

Its pipeline is ambitious—targeting both rare (i.e., ALS) and common diseases (i.e., radiation-induced xerostomia, Parkinson's).

Ahead of Phase 1 data for AAV-hAQP1 for radiation-induced xerostomia (expected on April 16), MeiraGTx’s stock has reached 52-week highs, now valued at nearly $1B (market capitalization).

Below, I take a look at the market opportunity in radiation-induced xerostomia, as well as other upcoming catalysts.

Radiation-Induced Xerostomia

The market for radiation-induced xerostomia is a bit tricky, which probably explains why the company went out of its way to say it will also “include a section on the drug’s commercial opportunity.”

The Phase 1 study is an open-label, non-randomized, dose escalation trial that enrolled 24 patients to primarily evaluate for AAV-hAQP1’s safety.

It delivers a single dose of AAV-hAQP1 into one or both parotid glands and observes the patients over years. AAV-hAQP1 has since advanced into a Phase 2 trial.

MeiraGTx revealed positive Phase 1 data in December 2022, which I have summarized in the table below.

Following the data, AAV-hAQP1 was awarded with RMAT and Breakthrough Therapy Designation. The latter was specifically supported by the 3-year data that will be fully unveiled to the public on Thursday.

Now, radiation-induced xerostomia is a common and debilitating side effect of radiation for head and neck cancers. The company is specifically targeting survivors who suffer from persistent Grade 2/3 radiation-induced xerostomia (fibrotic and non-functional). There is currently no approved curative treatment.

Although there are 170k survivors (estimate) with chronic Grade 2/3 radiation-induced xerostomia, as the company cites, I’d be wary of using this as a Target Pop. when modeling AAV-hAQP1.

In its Phase 1 and Phase 2 clinical trials, MeiraGTx enrolls “long-term” survivors and “mid-term” survivors, respectively.

There are roughly 70k new cases of HNC annually in the US, and about 30% will develop permanent, clinically significant xerostomia despite modern sparing techniques like IMRT. This gives AAV-hAQP1 a “new incident flow” of 21k. I also accounted for 1.5% annual Pop. Growth.

Again, TAM is up for interpretation, but I like to lean conservative and realistic.

To balance my conservatism, I also assume a 20% Yr 1 Uptake (which is very aggressive) and a 25% Peak Share. I set Probability of Success, or POS, at 45%, which is higher than the typical Phase 2 therapy. POS could increase further depending on how the data looks tomorrow. I have Launch Year at 2028, Peak Year at 2034, and LOE at 2040 (12-year biologic exclusivity). Net Price estimates could vary, but $200k leans conservative for a one-time “functional cure.” And, although xerostomia is debilitating, it isn’t "life or death." COGS is at 8%. Launch OpEx is 45% for 3 Yrs. Steady-State OpEx (after 3 Yrs of Launch) drops to 25%. I use a WACC discount rate of 12% to account for clinical-stage risk.

I have AAV-hAQP1 generating roughly $1.2B at peak, and worth $979.8M in risk-adjusted Net Present Value. And should further data increase POS to, say, 55%, its rNPV could feasibly jump over $1.2B.

Broader Pipeline Implications

AAV-hAQP1 is also being evaluated in Sjögren’s Syndrome, which is a much larger autoimmune indication that impacts as many as 4 million Americans. So, not only could tomorrow’s data de-risk radiation-induced xerostomia, but it could also help validate larger targets.

Like radiation-induced xerostomia, Sjögren’s is also a “water channel” problem. They share pathology (destroyed acinar cells). AAV-hAQP1 works because it inserts the hAQP1 gene, which creates a “pore” that allows water to move passively through these ductal cells.

MGTX Stock Financial Health

MeiraGTx ended the year with $65.9M in cash and investments.

While the company makes money from big pharma collaborations like Eli Lilly (LLY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), it is not enough to offset R&D and SG&A expenses. In fact, the company “burned” (cash from operations) $46.4M in 2025, giving them ~6 quarters of cash runway. MeiraGTx expects its cash runway to extend "into the second half of 2027."

In early 2026, MeiraGTx received an additional $55M from the Hologen collaboration in Parkinson’s. In March, there was an amendment of the Perceptive credit facility, which extended its debt to May 2027 ($25M due June 2026, $50M due May 2027).

So, although lumpy, the company does have some financial flexibility.

Still, I think there’s a good chance of an equity raise in the near future.

Conclusion

Tomorrow’s update is kind of a big deal for AAV-hAQP1 and MeiraGTx’s broader platform. Positive data (which seems quite likely, judging from Breakthrough Therapy Designation) could not only de-risk the potential ~$1.2B peak-sales xerostomia opportunity, but also help validate the same water-channel mechanism in the much larger Sjögren’s indication.

With vertically-integrated riboswitch-controlled tech and big pharma partnerships, I think MGTX, despite its recent run-up, offers significant upside (initiate with a "Buy" rating) for investors who can tolerate clinical-stage biotech volatility and risks (i.e., dilution, clinical trial failure, regulatory setbacks, etc.).

data.title: Vera Bradley: A Colorful Turnaround
data.description:
Vera Bradley (VRA) is not the first company that shot itself, and probably is not the last one. In 2024, the former CEO announced brand transformation and basically started to pivot from Vera Bradley's core identity. That upset the loyal customers, slashed sales, and led the stock to an all-time low. Although that transformation was not the only misstep, it was the final nail in the coffin.

In June 2025, the former CEO stepped down, and Ian Bickley was appointed as interim CEO and later as permanent CEO. Martin Layding was also appointed as CFO and COO, with Ivan Brockman as a member of the board. Bickley's retail experience in handbags and accessories combined with Layding's experience in PE-backed companies and Brockman's experience in investment banking suggests that the goal is to turn around the company and could even be to sell it.

The sale is also in the interest of the new management team due to the change in control terms in their contracts. Their options will be immediately vested (instead of a three-year vesting period), and they might walk away with up to 1.5x of their base salary as cash severance and pro-rated bonuses. However, even if the company is not sold, a successful turnaround could still offer a great opportunity.

Right after taking charge, Bickley introduced Project Sunshine to turn the company back to its core identity:

Over the past quarter, we have remained focused on delivering Project Sunshine which anchors on reclaiming Vera Bradley's joyful optimism and acts as our North Star, bringing creative energy to how we work within functions and across the Vera Bradley team. It is leading us to new ideas for products, marketing and channels, transforming how we work with each other and encouraging us to think differently about our operations.

At the same time, we have been addressing past missteps with urgency and implementing comprehensive changes across the business and organization, demonstrating the focus and agility of our team. To remind you, the 5 strategic pillars under Project Sunshine are: first, sharpening our brand focus through product relevance and storytelling. Second, resetting our go-to-market approach with data-led insights. Third, rewiring our digital ecosystem across all touch points. Fourth, implementing Outlet 2.0 for a more brand-enhancing retail experience. And fifth, reimagining how we work with new capabilities and organizational alignment.

To understand what the main goal of the turnaround is, we should start by understanding the core business.

Background

The fascinating story of Vera Bradley and its cofounders, Barbara Bradley and Pat Miller, can be found in the book "A Colorful Way of Living" and in a short documentary video of "The Vera Bradley Story." I also recommend watching this interview with Barbara Bradley.

This is basically how the company was created, by sewing patterns on a ping-pong table with an initial $500 of borrowed money:

While traveling on vacation in March of 1982, neighbors and friends Patricia R. Miller and Barbara Bradley Baekgaard were awaiting a flight in Atlanta when they noticed a lack of colorful, distinctive luggage.These dynamic women turned $500 into a company named after Barbara’s mother to design, manufacture and market colorful, high-quality handbags, luggage and accessories for women.

The cofounders have always wanted to give more color to life and make it more fun and joyful for people around them. This can be seen not only in Vera Bradley bags and accessories but also in their hotel and Breast Cancer Foundation. This attitude created a unique company culture and brought exceptionally loyal customers.

During the 2008 financial crisis, Vera Bradley was one of the few outperformers and was able to sustain 24% annual growth with great margins. In 2010, the company went through an IPO and was listed on Nasdaq. This is the financial performance of the business since its IPO:

Of note is that Vera Bradley acquired 75% of Pura Vida for $75M in 2019 and bought the remaining 25% for $10M in 2023. This turned out to be a horrible acquisition that increased costs and made the business unprofitable. Pura Vida was sold in 2025 for a total consideration of $3.5M (page 73 of the latest 10-K). Although the loss on their investment was significant, this divestiture was necessary to turn the company back to profitability.

After Vera Bradley went public, I think there were two major causes that eventually led the company to its current situation:

  1. The growth pressure and short-term thinking to hit revenue targets, which is always a challenge on Wall Street, resulted in overexpansion in products and stores. This overexpansion led management to introduce discounting cycles and basically train their customers to never pay the full price.
  2. As the company grew and cofounders got older, their control over business operations faded, which eventually resulted in losing the unique culture, brand identity, and loyal customers.

Now the question is, is there any hope for a successful turnaround?

Turnaround

As mentioned, there are 5 strategic pillars that the current management team has been pursuing. While it is still too soon to see the results of their actions on the financial results, I think there are some signs of improvement, especially in the efforts to sharpen the brand focus. They discontinued many of their products with a minimalist look and simplified SKUs. They also started to focus on storytelling in social media and increased brand awareness. As a result, the "Vera Bradley" search trend has shown positive growth over the last year:

Looking at the "Vera Bradley" search trend and VRA stock price since its IPO, it seems like the Google search trend has usually moved before the stock price and hence acted as a leading indicator.

Comparing FY2026 with FY2025, net revenues declined by 24% in Q1, 24.6% in Q2, 11.7% in Q3, and 1.7% in Q4. This clearly shows that momentum was created after the shift in company strategy. Gross margin declined more than 12% in Q3 (42% vs 54.5%) and improved by around 2% in Q4 (47.8% vs 45.9%). The decline in gross margin at the beginning of Project Sunshine was due to heavy reliance on clearance to sell the discontinued products. However, the management was able to stop the bleeding in Q4 and returned the company to profitability after 1.5 years.

Despite the strategic shift, the current return to profitability was mainly attributed to cost management. In FY2026, management reduced corporate expenses by $10.6M. However, revenue continued to decline in both direct and indirect segments.

One could argue that this decline is mainly due to intense competition and a change in customer taste. While these are some of the likely causes, I believe mismanagement was the primary reason. It would not be unexpected to see stabilized or even growing revenues with proper management. Additionally, the operating income of the core business shows why Vera Bradley can be an attractive target for a PE. By further reducing corporate expenses and focusing on the brand's core identity, the company can offer a decent ROI.

Another strategy that the new management is currently trying to implement is the scarcity sales model. For instance, look at this Facebook post for selling only 50 handcrafted bags:

I think this is a great idea that can change sentiment around the brand.

Valuation

Vera Bradley is currently trading below its book value. The company has no outstanding debt, $76M in inventory, and $18.5M in cash. It also owns its HQ property and a distribution center (around 430,000 square feet). The HQ was valued at $25M and listed for sale-leaseback in 2025 (the listing was later terminated). While the liquidation value of inventory is probably less than its book value and there are some costs associated with termination of the outstanding leases, I think it is fair to say that the company is currently trading close to its liquidation value. Therefore, just stopping cash burn can probably move the stock up.

Valuing a company in the middle of a turnaround is like art. The current numbers are usually awful, which can easily justify their current low valuation or even further slump. However, in case they can come back to growth and even partially return to the good old days, the upside is certainly huge. This, of course, takes time, and that is why it is usually too soon to jump in until it is too late. For instance, you can see what happened in Fossil Group (FOSL). Everything was looking awful for a few years, and then the new management got on board and started to fix things. However, the stock was still performing poorly for over a year until everything started to look better and the stock almost quintupled in a very short period of time.

The way I like to play this kind of situation is to stay on the sidelines before seeing some signs of improvement. This can push the stock up significantly and shrink the margin of safety, but these companies often experience a pullback or another round of pessimism that can give us what we want at a price we want. Vera Bradley has already shown some signs of improvement, and that is why the stock went up around 50% in less than a month after its earnings. Therefore, I believe we are in the third stage, which is waiting for a good entry point. If that opportunity never arrives, I would rather move on and go to the next opportunity than jump into something with an inadequate margin of safety.

Looking at the total revenues and margins in the last five years, I think a successful turnaround will easily bring up sales to $300M with total operating income of around $10M in two to three years. Since they have NOL from the Pura Vida divestiture, the operating income will be close to their net income. Assuming P/E = 10 and adding $18M of cash, the stock price would sit around $4.2 per share.

(($10M x 10) + $18M) / 28M Shares Outstanding = $4.2

I want at least a 15% return over 3 years. Therefore, the price at which I am comfortable jumping in is:

$4.2 / (1.15)^3 = $2.8

You can play around with any of those assumptions, but I think the overall approach is making sense for turnaround stories. As mentioned, it is very possible we never see that price again, but if we do, that would be the kind of opportunity I like.

Risk

The main problem with turnarounds is they usually fail! Many things have to work out in favor of the business to see a successful one. That is why it is important to have an adequate margin of safety. One thing I especially focus on in a turnaround thesis is the quality of management and whether they have similar experience in the past. In Vera Bradley, I think the current team is appropriate for the task, which increases the chance of success.

Competition is an important barrier that can challenge the operation. I think if management sticks to the current plan and focuses more on a scarcity model, they can bring back their loyal customers and find new ones. Storytelling is also key for this brand, and the current management seems to understand it well.

Conclusion

Vera Bradley has already shown some signs of improvement and indication of a successful turnaround. They have the right people in charge, a good story to tell, and unique and nostalgic products to sell. These might offer a great opportunity. However, anything can be a good investment at the right price and a horrible investment at the wrong price. I would wait for a price at which I think it is absolutely right to jump in. Until then, I will stay on hold!

data.title: Outlook For Copper As Supply And Demand Disrupted
data.description:
The Middle East conflict is raising fresh concerns about global growth while putting commodities and supply chain vulnerabilities back in the spotlight. Craig Hutchison, Director, Equity Research with TD Cowen, looks at what investors should watch in mining as demand cools, logistics face disruption, and pricing dynamics grow more uncertain.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Well, the conflict in the Middle East has had a wide-ranging impact on the commodities market. And it's not just supplies of oil and natural gas being disrupted. Joining us now to discuss what it means for base metals like copper is Craig Hutchison, Director for Equity Research with TD Cowen. And for full disclosure on the companies covered by TD Cowen, please see the link at the end of this video. Craig, great to have you on the show again.

Craig Hutchison: Yeah, thanks for having me back.

Anthony Okolie: All right, so let's talk about the Middle East and the impact it's having on base metals mining space. What are you seeing there?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. Volatile times we live in. Obviously, the war is having a major impact on energy supply. Energy supply is critical for global growth. So if you constrain that supply or the prices go up, as we've seen at the pump, and I'm sure everyone's experiencing it, that's going to be a headwind to global growth.

At the same time, there are other factors that I'm sure we can talk about it, some of the other impacts to supply itself. Sulfuric acid has been constrained. That's a major input to the extraction of copper. There's been a few operations in the Middle East that have also been constrained. The Middle East is not a major producer of copper. Iran has some small mines. But overall, there have been some offsetting factors that have helped the market be a little bit balanced in this environment.

Anthony Okolie: What about diesel? What's the sensitivity there?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. So we put out a piece on Friday, our Q1 preview, and we surveyed all the companies that we cover and asked them the question, what's the impact in the current price environment? And, by and large, most of the companies have assumed between $60 and $80 WTI or Brent at the start of the year. So every $10 move in the diesel price -- or the oil price, I should say -- has about a $0.02 to $0.05 impact on the cost structure.

It depends on where the mines are located, depends on the type of mine. So, for example, Capstone, which has open-pit mines, which, as you can imagine, rely heavily on diesel for the movement of trucks and shovels, they're probably looking at somewhere around $0.03 a pound for every $10 move. But, on average, you're probably looking at, at today's prices, maybe about a $0.10 to $0.15 impact on cash costs.

There are some offsetting factors as well. Most of the companies produce some precious metals, whether it's gold or silver. And the precious metal prices have obviously moved very high. And for the most part, guides are assuming around $3,900 per ounce gold prices. And today, we're sitting at $4,700, $4,800 an ounce. So those benefits from the higher revenues in gold and silver will actually offset a lot of the impact on diesel. But it definitely is a cost headwind.

Anthony Okolie: Now, obviously, with higher prices, it's going to have an impact on the global economy and, of course, global growth. So how will those headwinds for global growth impact the supply and demand for copper?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. So we rely heavily on Wood Mackenzie, which is a subscription we pay for, which follows the industry. And they've reset their expectations already to reflect the higher energy prices. So going into the war, they were expecting around 2.6% demand growth. Now they've ratcheted that down to around 2.2%.

So on their numbers, we have, I would say, a fairly balanced market -- about a 300-ton deficit, which, in the context of around a 27 million ton market, is fairly balanced, but slightly on the deficit side. Interestingly, and maybe we could talk about just the Ivanhoe situation as well, there have been some disruptions on the supply side, which have actually impacted the numbers further. So it's actually made the market a little bit tighter. I think the market's probably closer to 400,000 tons of deficit at this point.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So, given that backdrop, what's the health of the miners right now? And what are you expecting in the upcoming earnings season?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. So Q1, I think, is going to be a softer quarter. There are seasonal factors that drive the lower production this quarter. We saw similar seasonality in 2025. So seasonality could be weather-related impacts. Obviously, we've had a tough winter here in the Northeast, so guys who have operations in the northern climates have experienced a lot of snow, and that impacts production.

Grade profiles -- Q1, for whatever reason, this year round, and similar to last year, has lower grades. And as you get into higher grades, you get higher production. Other factors would just include ramp-ups. Teck has their QB mine that's getting better and better every day. Capstone had a strike, and one of their biggest mines is ramping up as well. So you'll see stronger production throughout the year.

But going into Q1, I think it'll be the softest quarter from a production standpoint. But at the same time, we also have record copper prices and gold prices in the quarter. So earnings should be strong. Balance sheets, for most of the producers, are all in good shape. We've gone through a period here of nice earnings for the last couple of years, so no one's in a liquidity crisis. But we'll see stronger, I think, earnings and production growth as we come out of Q1.

Original Post

data.title: HumanX 2026 AI Conference Takeaways: The Gap Is Widening
data.description:
By Zeno Mercer

I just got back from HumanX, the enterprise AI conference held last week in San Francisco. Now in its second year, HumanX has already hit a remarkable milestone: over 6,500 attendees and hundreds of speakers, ranging from Al Gore, to Fei-Fei Li (widely known as the godmother of AI), and Daniela Rus, director of MIT’s CSAIL and strategic advisor for the ROBO Global Index suite.

Zeno Mercer (front right) at Daniela Rus’ (center) more intimate roundtable discussion that occurred right after her panel “Building AI Systems That Sense, Decide, and Act” at HumanX 2026.

The Biggest Takeaway?

That there are now two types of companies in the world. The first treats headcount and agentic workflows + token usage/optimization as equally important inputs into how the business runs. The second is still in discovery, without a real AI strategy. The gap between them is widening by the quarter.

To leaders across the world, whether it’s executive leadership, government, academic institution, or nonprofit, it is time to lock in. Others already have.

If your AI strategy is rolling out Gemini or Copilot seats and tracking license utilization, you are measuring the wrong thing. The step change is not seat count. It is whether your organization knows how to design, deploy, and manage agentic workflows, including for roles that were previously considered “non-technical”.

Look at how Jack Dorsey is rebuilding Block (XYZ) as an AI-first company. As an internal AI agent that now handles roughly 90% of code submissions, headcount came down 40%, and guidance went up. That is what an operating model built around agents looks like, not a dashboard counting chat prompts. It is also the direction that we will eventually see the majority of companies head - industry and context depending - over the coming years.

The Big 3 Takeaways

  1. Trust (and budget, and access to compute) is the bottleneck, not capability. Twenty-three panels centered on the disconnect between what AI can already do and what organizations are willing to let it do. Procurement decisions worth billions are being made without adequate evaluation frameworks. Ion Stoica of the LMSYS team named it the “trust gap” on the reliability panel. Only about 40% of AI vendors are making it into production at Fortune 500 companies today. This is a governance and cultural problem, not a technical one.
  2. Agents crossed from demos to production. Agentic AI appeared across 79 panel links. The production signals are concrete. Vercel reports 30% of deployments are now driven by agents with 167% year-over-year growth. Ramp’s CTO said 60% of pull requests merged in the prior week came from their coding agent. Tom Eggemeier, CEO of Zendesk, said his top customer resolves 92% of customer interactions through an AI agent. Uber’s CTO Praveen Naga pegged AI-generated code at roughly 70% of new commits. Salesforce is calling agents the “digital workforce” with intent.
  3. Physical AI emerged as the next wave. NVIDIA’s Jensen Huang explicitly framed physical AI as “wave three” of this cycle. Multiple panels covered world models (Runway, World Labs, Black Forest Labs), sim-to-real transfer, and autonomous systems that extend well past self-driving. Skild AI presented an omnibodied-intelligence thesis: one model, any body type. As the research lead covering Robotics and AI for the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO) and the ROBO Global Artificial Intelligence Index (THNQ), I dare say this is the thread that most directly compounds the next decade.

Governance vs Speed, the P&G / Harvard Study Leaders Cannot Ignore

Seth Cohen of Procter & Gamble presented a study that P&G ran with Harvard Business School across 700 employees. Four groups (teams, teams with AI, individuals, individuals with AI) were each asked to generate new product innovation ideas, blind-judged by experts.

The teams who had access to AI performed the best. But a very close second was not one of the teams without access to AI. It was the individuals who had access to AI. The teams and individuals without AI came in a very distant third and fourth place.

Read that again. A single person with AI beat an entire team without AI.

Which is precisely why governance-as-drag is now a strategic risk. Typeface’s research, cited on the Creativity at AI Speed panel, found that 67% of marketers miss cultural moments because of slow review and approval cycles. On a separate panel, enterprise buyers described hard usage caps with approval queues attached, and the admission that “you’re punishing your performers.”

The lesson for leaders: governance is necessary, but governance that kneecaps your best individual contributors goes beyond strategic self-sabotage, to potentially existential, organizational apoptosis off the world scale.

So - what are the companies that are using AI worried about?

A Deeper Dive into the State of Compute

The clearest moment of the week came from Dr. Fei-Fei Li on the opening night mainstage. Bloomberg’s Ed Ludlow asked how much of World Labs’ fresh one billion dollar raise went to Jensen Huang. Her answer was an instant: “And Lisa, right?”

The room laughed. It was one of the sharper two-word summaries of the compute market all week. Frontier model companies are not single-vendor buyers. NVIDIA (NVDA) and AMD are both in every serious build order. Sumit Sadana of Micron (MU) framed it the same way on the Silicon Shift panel.

Albeit pre-recorded from a week prior, Jensen chimed in with his own take (which was also the framing of an entire panel series), which is that the industry is a five-layer stack: power, chips, infrastructure, models, applications.

Bryan Catanzaro of NVIDIA, on the “5 Layer Cake” panel, added that there are now four scaling laws driving compute demand simultaneously: pre-training, post-training, deployment-time reasoning, and agents. All four are energy-bounded.

Hunger Games for Compute

From Nebius (NBIS), a sovereign AI cloud provider and THNQ constituent that has been pulling significant hyperscaler partnership flow, to Super Micro Computer (SMCI), a THNQ holding and a key hardware partner for xAI’s Colossus build-out in Memphis, the demand curve for training and increasingly inference compute (what we can fairly call “the token economy”) was visible in every side conversation. Rodrigo Liang of SambaNova put it plainly: “hyperscalers, neoclouds, sovereign clouds, large-scale enterprises” are all running data centers for token factories now.

The Token Economics

On pricing, Sam Altman was repeatedly cited (by other speakers, on the Business Reality of AI Adoption panel, among others) as the clearest voice signaling that usage-based, metered pricing is where consumer and enterprise AI is going. The economic logic is simple. Power is scarce. Flat-rate pricing in a power-scarce world is unsustainable for the provider. Enterprise sales teams at some companies are already consuming 1B tokens per month. Together AI, speaking on the open-vs-closed panel, reported growth from 10 billion tokens per day in early 2025 to over 5 trillion tokens per day now. Three orders of magnitude in roughly a year on a single platform.

Jensen put the new unit of economic output in one phrase: “tokens per watt.” Every data center has to optimize for it. Every architecture has to be co-designed for it. This also, from my perspective, expands into local, open-source AI that can run on everyday hardware, similar to how the personal AI “Openclaw economy” is taking off - I’ll go as far as to say it’s a combination of the PC and smartphone moment for AI.

Matt Garman, CEO of AWS (AMZN), opened the agentic-inflection panel on the twentieth anniversary of AWS (Pi Day). His framing: agents are how most enterprises will capture most of their AI value, and AWS is building agent-first infrastructure accordingly.

Part II: The Agentic Economy

Beyond pure inference, a large slice of HumanX was builders focused on the agentic layer itself. Agentic harnesses, token optimization, and what one speaker called the “battlefield of the future” for enterprise workflow.

Speed matters (sub-second tool calls, inference optimized for throughput not latency, as Impala AI’s cofounder put it: “we treat it like an HPC problem, not a web service”). Databases matter (Snowflake (SNOW), Datadog (DDOG), and the data plane plays). Agentic web search is a new category of its own, with Bright Data, Tavily, You.com, Brave Search, Exa, and Perplexity all competing to be the default retrieval layer for AI agents. For others, it’s not speed, but cost (especially as we see gigantic data loads for multi-modal physical AI training).

The trust stack sits underneath all of it. Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) for enterprise security. Cloudflare (NET) running stablecoin rails for agent-to-agent payments, which is early but real.

Bottom Line Takeaways

Jensen put the unit of economic output in one phrase: tokens per watt. Every operator in the room was already pricing around it. In a power-scarce world, metered usage is the only honest way to sell it.

In robotics and physical AI, the metric expands. You still track tokens per watt, and you also have to answer for useful actions per joule, sensor fidelity per dollar, actuation cycles before drift, manipulations per fault, and uptime per operator. The same discipline, pushed through the reality of physics: friction, latency, heat, and power constraints that no model can pretend away once it is driving a body.

That is why this new cycle holds together at the index level. Digital AI and Physical AI are two substrates of the same story. ROBO and THNQ sit across the stack it runs on: the silicon and photonics that turn watts into useful cycles, the clouds and sovereign compute that meter them, the data and trust layers that keep them legible, and the agents and world models that turn capacity into outcomes.

Disclaimers

ROBO is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO), the L&G ROBO Global Robotics and Automation UCITS ETF (ROBO.LN), and the Global X ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO.AU). THNQ is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Artificial Intelligence ETF (THNQ) and the L&G Artificial Intelligence UCITS ETF (AIAI.LN).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com
