data.title: Nvidia And AMD: Trim Your Hare, Buy A Tortoise
data.description:
I address this article directly to Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) bulls who are patiently waiting for the stock to break through a very crucial resistance level of $200, which the stock has been unable to do since it briefly crossed to the upside in November 2025. At $196, that time may soon be nigh, but over that time-window, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and NVDA have been leapfrogging over each other. One should note that although both stocks are down by mid to high single digit percentages, AMD's periodic bounce-backs have exceeded those of the incumbent data center and AI GPU leader.

Last month, I penned an NVDA-exclusive piece that outlined the opportunity cost that was accruing to shareholders, and despite the stock being up 14 percent since March 30, it still lingers in the mid-$190s as of this writing.

For context, and so the title does not confuse readers, I still maintain my Strong Sell on NVDA, but my rating is based on trimming the holding down to the extent that it matches an AMD component on a 1:1 basis by share count. One should note that a dollar-equivalent match will not yield the same results because one would be lower-weighted to AMD due to its higher share price. Mathematically, $1000 in NVDA and $1000 in AMD will get you (at current prices) 5.28 NVDA and 4.05 AMD, and that is not what one wants. However, one can choose to stay flexible based on whether to increase or decrease risk.

Going back a little further, in February, I wrote a piece urging NVDA shareholders to pair their holding with AMD as a risk-mitigation strategy, and this article is a continuation of that thesis. I shall show how an equal pairing backtested over the April 2025 to March 2026 period offered better risk-adjusted returns than a holding of either NVDA or AMD exclusively.

My purpose with this article is multi-fold. Backtesting does help validate a thesis, but it is only the tip of the iceberg. The underlying dynamic of each stock being vastly divergent, it is even more important to look at how this story might play out over the next year or more.

The bigger picture I would like to paint, however, is the Hare and Tortoise race that these two giants of data center and AI GPUs appear to be engaged in. While NVDA's strategy is to remain on the bleeding edge of AI compute, AMD's broader ecosystem strategy involves capturing clients from the gaps left by NVDA in terms of pricing, availability, and cost of ownership.

To be precise, it is not just these two companies vying for a meaningful share of the AI pie; the hyperscalers with their big CapExes and native AI silicon play an equal role in the long run. However, in the context of NVDA and AMD, I believe one should hold both stocks in their portfolio in equal measure. One again, I reiterate that my Strong Sell for NVDA means "trim your position," not
sell whatever you own." It is increasingly becoming urgent that one does this if one's NVDA weighting overwhelms every other stock in one's portfolio, as is the case with many of my readers here on Seeking Alpha.

Before we get into the "why" of it, I shall reveal the results of my backtest.

The Backtest and its Implications

The selected period for this backtest runs from April 2025 through March 2026 - the post-Liberation-Day-tariff era. Why 1 year, and why this specific period? I chose it not only because it is the most recent and meaningful period for comparison, but also because last April marked a significant trough in the broader market, so I see it as a good starting point. A longer time-window would obviously favor NVDA because of its historically stronger price action, and a shorter one would not capture the post-tariff market reaction and subsequent recovery.

We shall now see how three portfolios have performed over the period in question: an equal NVDA-AMD mix, an NVDA-only portfolio, and an AMD-only portfolio.

One can clearly see the balance that the mixed portfolio brings - it tempers the drawdown of the worse-performing stock and makes the portfolio less volatile, and in exchange, is able to maintain relatively high risk-adjusted return ratios like Sharpe and Sortino.

Of course, one could argue that an AMD-only semi portfolio will have outperformed the mixed approach over the last one year, and that an NVDA-only one might outperform it in future time-windows, but my belief is that reducing risk is an equally important consideration alongside capturing upside potential. With the mixed portfolio, one still captures upside, but one also buffers the portfolio against violent drawdowns and high volatility in either stock.

I strongly believe that this makes even more sense now, because we seem to be at a stage where oil prices could persist at these levels for the next six months, at the very least.

Specific to the U.S. market, March 2026 inflation figures seem to reflect this sentiment, with headline inflation up 0.9 percent to 3.3 percent, primarily driven by energy prices:

The index for energy rose 10.9 percent in March, led by a 21.2-percent increase in the index for gasoline which accounted for nearly three quarters of the monthly all items increase. The shelter index also increased in March, rising 0.3 percent. The index for food was unchanged over the month as the index for food away from home rose 0.2 percent, while the index for food at home fell 0.2 percent.

Food and Shelter CPI increases are lagging indicators, so I would not be surprised by a higher print on those in the months ahead.

All of this points to a market still in shock, and even though the S&P 500 Index (SP500) is up nearly 16 percent over the period under discussion, questions persist around where it is headed by year-end.

NVDA the Hare Vs AMD the Tortoise

There are three specific angles I would like to explore in this article:

  1. Development Speeds for the Respective GPU Roadmaps - The Sprinter versus The Marathoner.
  2. The Ecosystem Build-out - The CUDA Golden Cage versus The Multi-Vendor Open Highway.
  3. The Target Market Differential - The Haves versus The Have-Nots.

Development Speed: The Sprinter Versus The Marathoner

NVDA's product launch cadence has perceptibly accelerated, from 36 months for the Volta to Ampere jump, to the expected 12 months from Blackwell to Rubin. One should note that the above table does not capture the Blackwell delays, which pushed the rollout to 2025, and CEO Huang at the CES confirmed "full production" for Rubin, which is quite odd considering the move to Taiwan Semi's (TSM) 3nm nodes will require time to validate and ramp to full production. If Huang is correct in saying that "Some of Nvidia’s partners have started running their next-generation AI models on early Rubin systems," the jump from Blackwell to Rubin can be confirmed at less than one year.

The problem with moving to a yearly rollout is at the heart of what Michael Burry was saying last year about prolonged depreciation artificially boosting earnings for hyperscalers and enterprises. The key point for investors here is that it is these very earnings that validate NVDA stock, so if they are in doubt, so is NVDA's own valuation. Naturally, that view has been argued against ad nauseum, and nothing has changed on the accounting front, so I believe that dynamic might still be in play.

What concerns me even more is something I pointed to in my earlier NVDA articles - that these hyperscalers are increasing their indebtedness in their attempt to be the first to grab anything that comes out of NVDA's proverbial door.

The CapEx increases across the board of hyperscalers is putting considerable pressure on their respective balance sheets, and although the full effect on free cash flows is yet to materialize, one sees them starting to come under pressure. I see it getting worse from here, and that is essentially NVDA's biggest risk likely to get even bigger.

These factors should be viewed through the lens of potentially higher or even stable interest rates, how that affects debt burdens, how much spending elasticity these hyperscalers have, where these CapEx monies are being directed, and how the overall AI supercycle shapes up in terms of return on capital. It could get worse before it gets better.

To be fair, AMD is no different when it comes to product cadence.

One sees the same pattern-shift to a yearly rollout that echoes NVDA's own drum beat, but AMD's situation is different because its price point puts it in a different market segment from NVDA, as I shall discuss later.

The gap left by NVDA due to its higher pricing and persistent lack of availability for the non-hyperscaler and non-neocloud market has prompted many enterprises (including hyperscalers) to opt for AMD's rack-scale solutions. It is also imperative that AMD match up to NVDA's product rollout, which I see as a positive for the former.

On a tangential note, while NVDA's demand has been pegged at upwards of $1 trillion through 2027, HBM4 shortages now threaten these projections. For AMD, this could be as much of a threat, but its recent deal with Samsung (SSNLF) for the latter to produce HBM4 could help offset the fact that SK Hynix has already sold out its inventory through 2026, largely to NVDA. That implies greater risk for NVDA in 2027 and beyond, because the memory bottleneck is now expected last through 2030 if demand remains the same.

Even under a best-case scenario, memory supply is expected to persist through 2027, with most expansion plans by Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron only coming online by next year, and that brings with it execution and supply chain risks. For AMD, the risk is more subdued because Samsung has already begun mass production of its HBM4 offering. This may afford it an advantage over NVDA in the near to medium term, but I should reiterate that the risk to AMD is not fully ameliorated yet.

Coming back to the development cadence for AI accelerators and rack-scale offerings, I believe AMD has a distinct edge because it is not yet in full-on competition mode against NVDA, although this may be its ultimate goal. In the meantime, AMD's data center revenues continue to grow near the 40 percent mark. As of Q4 2025:

Data Center segment revenue in the quarter was a record $5.4 billion, up 39% year-over-year, driven by strong demand for AMD EPYC™ processors and the continued ramp of AMD Instinct GPU shipments.

For the full year 2025, Data Center segment revenue was a record $16.6 billion, up 32% year-over-year, reflecting growth across both EPYC CPUs and AMD Instinct GPUs.

These revenues are essentially NVDA's missed opportunities, as I see it, and this is the biggest difference between the two companies: NVDA is on an accelerated product path because it needs to stay at the top of the data center GPU game, while AMD's accelerated product cadence is primarily targeting the gaps left by the incumbent leader in both the hyperscaler and enterprise spaces. As for hyperscalers, I see their relationships with AMD as hedges against high CapEx going toward NVDA's more expensive rack-scale solutions.

There is a curious dynamic at play here that introduces a unique kind of valuation risk.

NVDA's currently depressed valuations (relative to AMD's) could see a multiple expansion if revenue and earnings growth slow down and the price does not. The current compression is due to its price remaining stubbornly under $200 while revenue and earnings are growing in double and triple digits.

Should this happen, NVDA's multiples would rapidly expand. Ironically, this multiple expansion, then, can be considered a risk where one would normally look at it as a positive. The reason here is, surprisingly, interest rates. If the current inflation driven by persistent high oil prices I discussed above forces the Fed's hand and results in a rate-hike regime, one would need to use a higher discount rate on future cash flows for the same rate of return, which means intrinsic value would drop. Whatever exit multiple you apply to those lower-value earnings would result in a lower fair price, essentially devaluing the stock even though its price may not have moved.

This may not be an immediate risk, of course, but an important consideration as the economy navigates troubled waters, pardon the Hormuz pun. If NVDA begins to rally when rates are on the rise, the devaluation could be significant.

The key takeaway for our sprinter versus marathoner analogy is that AMD is going where NVDA cannot and/or does not want to go, and I see that as a much more sustainable, albeit slower path to long-term growth. Besides, NVDA scaling up to a 12-month cadence affects its own top line because by the time Blackwell is more widely available, Rubin will already be the go-to GPU, so the demand pull from the high-CapEx cohort is being artificially drawn away from Blackwell, and this cycle will persist for the foreseeable future, in my opinion. Of course, the obvious flaw in that argument is that Blackwell demand will come from elsewhere, but the question is, will the lower-CapEx cohort choose an older chip when AMD is able to offer a next-gen chip at a similar price point and performance? Perhaps not, in my view, and this is why the sprinter versus marathoner analogy rings true for me.

Ecosystem Build-out - The CUDA Golden Cage versus The Multi-Vendor Open Highway

I hinted at this in the previous section, but I would like to dive in to what I think is another of NVDA's big weaknesses and, therefore, AMD's strength.

The Golden Cage analogy has been used in parallel with "Golden Handcuffs" and "Gilded Cage" in a different context (and one that I do not find to be particularly objective). The context in which I am using it is the strong hold that NVDA has over its clients. If they want to stay on top of the AI training game (the gold), they need to invest in NVDA's products and its ecosystem (the cage).

The company's DGX/HGX, DGX Cloud, CUDA and other components of NVDA's ecosystem effectively lock clients in, and in my view, AMD's approach is more sustainable in the long run because the focus is on open-source interoperability and code-porting out of CUDA. This represents a larger effort by enterprises as well as hyperscalers to collaborate, diversify, and develop in-house solutions - all in an attempt to achieve terminal velocity and escape NVDA's gravity. It is a monopoly by any definition or standard, and one that is being attacked on multiple sides, which is precisely why it poses a greater risk to investors.

This snapshot excerpt from TillCode is quite revealing:

In NVDA's case for CUDA, these keywords stand out for me: "cutting-edge AI research," "mature ecosystem," "extensive community support," "best-optimized performance," and "significant CUDA investment and expertise."

These are the dominant features that allow NVDA to hold "90%+ market share in data center GPUs for AI workloads."

However, the stand-out keywords for ROCm are even more compelling from a long-term viewpoint: "Budget is a primary concern," "strong price-to-performance," "large memory capacity," "prioritize open-source...vendor independence," "where ROCm performs competitively," "portable code...multiple GPU vendors," "primarily compute-bound rather than dependent on specialized libraries."

Where is the evidence that these aspects are more compelling? For one, the momentum of developer adoption is accelerating for AMD's solutions:

But in 2025, things have changed:

  • PyTorch 3.1 offers native ROCm support for training and inference.
  • Popular libraries like DeepSpeed and Hugging Face Accelerate have added AMD-specific performance flags.
  • AMD-backed startups are developing middleware that translates CUDA workloads into ROCm-compatible formats.
  • OpenXLA and MLIR integrations have made AMD a first-class target for compiler-based model optimization.

And this is putting a significant amount of pressure on NVDA's client base (emphasis in original):

While Nvidia still dominates high-end training for LLMs like GPT-4o and Claude 3, AMD is making significant inroads in inference and mid-scale training. These workloads represent 70–80% of enterprise AI activity, which means the financial upside is massive.

Nvidia’s response has been to double down on the Blackwell architecture, introduce NVLink Switch Systems, and increase its DGX as-a-service offerings. But for customers prioritizing flexibility, transparency, and cost-efficiency, AMD’s model is more appealing.

What’s more, AMD is now co-designing systems with OEMs like Supermicro, Dell, and Lenovo—making it easier for traditional enterprises to deploy GPU clusters in on-prem or hybrid environments.

What appears to be happening is that developers are still largely locked into CUDA, but the winds of change are shifting in favor of more affordable, open-source, and CapEx-lighter solutions that AMD's offerings represent.

I shall be the first to admit that this is not going to be an overnight shift, but one cannot deny that NVDA's dominance is being attacked on several fronts, and AMD is one of the biggest beneficiaries of NVDA's downside risks.

Target Market Differential - The Haves versus The Have-Nots

One should have been able to grasp the gist of my thesis by now, but what all of these elements distil down to is the fact that NVDA serves the "Haves," while AMD is quietly sweeping up the "Have-nots" and the "wannabes" of AI workloads.

We have already seen how this trend is palpable in the way NVDA's key clients are diversifying - not just with AMD but with their own silicon. Companies like Alphabet and Amazon are already hitting significant milestones with their ASIC solutions, and while the trainiums and TPUs of AI compute still have a ways to go before they are able to catch up to the market leader in data center GPUs for AI, growth rates based on rapid ASIC adoption at the enterprise and hyperscaler level are showing strong trends:

AI Server Compute ASIC market valued at $31.2 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $193.5 billion by 2035, growing at a CAGR of 20.1% during the forecast period (2026-2035). The global AI server compute ASIC market is advancing as hyperscale data centers and enterprise cloud providers accelerate investment in specialized hardware designed to support increasingly complex artificial intelligence workloads. Demand is rising as organizations deploy dedicated compute accelerators capable of delivering higher processing efficiency and lower power consumption compared to traditional GPU-based architectures. The expansion of generative AI, natural language processing, and large-scale recommendation engines is pushing infrastructure providers to adopt custom ASIC solutions optimized for high-performance model training and inference. As AI models grow and computational requirements, the need for purpose-built silicon capable of handling massive parallel processing workloads continues to intensify.

Naturally, one could assume that this will impact AMD as much as NVDA, but AMD is working in parallel with hyperscalers to support AI instances on their native cloud platforms. While this does not necessarily pose a clear and present danger to NVDA's current dominance, it does allow AMD to aid hyperscalers in diversifying out of the CUDA ecosystem.

A good analogy here would be that NVDA is sitting atop a mountain, the big-CapEx cohort is burrowing its way into the base of that mountain, and AMD is collecting the landslide revenue that is being shaken out of it.

In Closing

To sum up what I have laid out above, I believe AMD will be a longer-term beneficiary in the AI race due to several factors, the most important of which are a product cadence that matches NVDA's, a greater focus on open standards, and an active targeting to budget-conscious AI workloads from a client cohort that has significant cross-connotations with NVDA's own concentrated client base.

From an investment perspective, I still rate NVDA a Strong Sell, but with the caveat that this implies trimming down bloated positions and matching it with an equal measure of AMD, which I continue to rate a Strong Buy.

As I approach the end of this article, NVDA has rallied to $196 as part of its April rally. However, my thesis is not based on short-term movements in price but significantly longer time-windows where it has room to play out. To NVDA bulls, I say this: appreciate the risks and act accordingly.

data.title: BioMarin Pharmaceutical's Rare Disease Portfolio Supplemented By Amicus Acquisition Makes A Buy
data.description:
This is my fourth BioMarin (BMRN) article following 01/2025's "BioMarin Pharmaceuticals: Share Downdraft Combined With VOXZOGO Potential Makes A Buy". In the interim BioMarin has dropped >12%. In this article I evaluate its progress over the intervening period and rate its current investment merits.

BioMarin has a solid rare disease franchise.

Overview

In its latest 10-K BioMarin's table of total revenues and disaggregated net product revenues set out below provides a useful rundown of its most recently reported revenues:

This table shows a nice spread of revenue generating products the bulk of which are showing modest near term revenue gains.

VOXZOGO (vosoritide) in treatment of achondroplasia

As described in its 10-K VOXZOGO is a once daily injection; it is approved for marketing in both the US and Japan for the treatment of achondroplasia in children with open growth plates of all ages. In the EU its treatment is limited to children four months and older; its age limits in other ex-US markets varies from place to place.

Slide 12 below from BioMarin's Q4/2025 slide deck provides both a VOXZOGO revenue bar chart and updates on its 2026 development plans:

I urge special attention to the following extract from the bottom right of this slide:

  • sNDA submission for full approval of VOXZOGO expected Q2’26, leveraging extensive safety and efficacy data package

As noted in the FDA release announcing approval of VOXZOGO. it:

...approved Voxzogo under the accelerated approval pathway, which allows for earlier approval of drugs that treat serious conditions and fill an unmet medical need, based on a surrogate or intermediate clinical endpoint. A condition of this accelerated approval is a post-marketing study that will assess final adult height.

Two recent articles include available data to support BioMarin's VOXZOGO's sNDA; these include:

  1. an 08/02/2021 article "Safe and persistent growth-promoting effects of vosoritide in children with achondroplasia: 2-year results from an open-label, phase 3 extension study" reporting that vosoritide treatment has safe and persistent growth-promoting effects in children with achondroplasia treated daily for two years, with annualized growth velocity increased from 4.26 cm/year at baseline to 5.39 cm/year at 52 weeks and 5.52 cm/year at week 104;
  2. an 07/15/2025 article "Vosoritide (Voxzogo) for Achondroplasia: A Review of Clinical and Real-World Evidence" reporting that VOXZOGO increases annualized growth velocity [AGV] by approximately 1.5-2.0 cm/year compared to placebo with sustained growth over 7 years, plus improvements in body proportions and craniofacial development.

During its Q4/2025 earnings call Chief R&D Officer Friberg pointed to slide 14 below in support of its anticipated submission for full approval:

He went on to note that its submission would:

...present a comprehensive story around final adult height as well as a broader health and wellness outcome data set. We have collected over 10,000 patient years of safety data, including data for some children who have been on Voxzogo treatment for more than 10 years underscoring the value of Voxzogo across all age groups, including meaningful impacts on quality of life and anatomic outcomes.

In addition to linear growth, Voxzogo's broad evidence package demonstrates the treatment's impact on key complications associated with achondroplasia, including foramen magnum stenosis and symptomatic spinal stenosis as well as improvements in body proportionality, arm span and leg deformities like genu varum and tibial bowing. These extensive data also show clear functional benefits with improvements in measures of mobility, gait and quality of life. Together, these findings support our plans to submit the full approval package to the FDA...

There can be no doubt that BioMarin's sNDA will be supported by a strong data package; this should significantly reduce the risk for any hiccups as the review process proceeds.

VOXZOGO (vosoritide) ancillary trials

Bad news for BioMarin bulls came on 03/16/2026 when BioMarin announced:

Following the occurrence of several slipped capital femoral epiphysis [SCFE] events in two ongoing investigated-sponsored trials, BioMarin has decided to discontinue enrollment and dosing of VOXZOGO (vosoritide) in children with Turner syndrome, SHOX deficiency and aggrecan (ACAN) deficiency in our Phase 2 CANOPY clinical trials.

At the same time BioMarin advised that no such issues had arisen in regard to children with Noonan syndrome, as well as those living with idiopathic short stature (ISS) without ACAN deficiency. Accordingly trials for these latter conditions are continuing. Similarly it advised:

SCFE events have not been observed in the Phase 2 BioMarin trials in these same conditions, nor have any cases been observed in the more than 5,000 infants and children who have received VOXZOGO for achondroplasia, which includes 10 years of clinical research and over 10,000 patient-years of safety data in clinical studies and post-marketing surveillance. There have similarly been no observed cases of SCFE in our clinical trials for hypochondroplasia.

This setback should reduce potential VOXZOGO Total Addressable Patient Population [TAPP] of 420,000 by:

  1. ~65,000 — Turner syndome;
  2. ~40,000 — SHOX deficiency;
  3. ~20,000 — Idiopathic Short Stature [ISS] with ACAN deficiency.

This cuts into a market opportunity estimated by BioMarin at $5 billion by ~30%, reducing it to ~$3.5 billion.

VOXZOGO competition

On 03/02/2026 Ascendis Pharma (ASND) received FDA approval for its YUVIWEL in treatment of achondroplasia. Contrast the opening page of its label below:

with that for VOXZOGO below

YUVIWEL is a weekly injection for children aged 2 and older. VOXZOGO is a daily injection with no specific age limit. In its Q4/2025 earnings call CCO Hubbard advised that BioMarin was leaning into this differentiation; she noted:

Since we believe Voxzogo will be the only approved therapy in this age segment for the next several years, driving new patient starts in children under 2 is a key pillar of our growth strategy. With the potential full approval of Voxzogo on the horizon, we believe families and caregivers will have even greater confidence in Voxzogo's established safety and efficacy profile when considering treatment for their infants and children with achondroplasia.

As one with a close family member who has taken an injected biologic with varying injection frequencies, I can vouch for the significance of weekly vs daily injections. Insofar as VOXZOGO has the more burdensome daily injection compared to YUVIWEL's weekly requirement, it is imperative that it differentiate itself positively in some other way; otherwise it is sure to struggle in the marketplace.

Areas where VOXZOGO has the edge include its:

  1. early starting age;
  2. years of successful real world experience;
  3. thousands of patients safely treated;
  4. proven safety profile;
  5. worldwide approval;
  6. potential for near term full approval.

Whether these will win out over the strong patient convenience of weekly vs daily injections is a story that will play out over the next several quarters.

Enzyme therapies

BioMarin's Q4/2025 slide deck slide 11 below provides a bar chart overview with a side panel discussion of its enzyme therapy franchise:

Good news for BioMarin bulls came on 02/27/2026 when it announced the the FDA had approved its PALYNZIQ sNDA extending its label to include adolescents. This was important because it allowed teens with phenylketonuria [PKU] to discard the ultra-restrictive diet required for PKU management.

During its presentation at the 03/26 Barclays conference CFO Mueller advised that while this will help cement ongoing PALYNZIQ growth it will not impact 2026 revenues because of drawn out titration protocals.

ROCTAVIAN

In my Share Downdraft article, I characterized its ROCTAVIAN (valoctocogene roxaparvovec-rvox) gene therapy approved to treat severe hemophilia A as a heavy disappointment. Unable to find a buyer for this therapy, in 02/23/2026 it announced that it would voluntarily remove ROCTAVIAN from the market.

BioMarin ups its game with bold acquisition of Amicus Therapeutics.

On 12/19/2025 BioMarin announced its deal to acquire Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD) for $14.50 per share in an all-cash transaction for a total equity value of approximately $4.8 billion. The deal seemed rich with its:

  • 33% premium to Amicus' last close;
  • 46% premium to the 30-day volume-weighted average stock price; and
  • 58% premium to the 60-day volume-weighted average stock price.

As an all cash deal it forced BioMarin to borrow. As announced in its deal release:

The transaction is not subject to financing conditions. BioMarin intends to finance the transaction through a combination of cash on hand and approximately $3.7 billion of non-convertible debt financing. Morgan Stanley Senior Funding, Inc. is acting as sole lead arranger and has provided a bridge commitment for this amount. The permanent financing structure will include a meaningful portion of pre-payable debt, in line with BioMarin's commitment to deleveraging with a target of gross leverage of <2.5x within two years after the closing of the proposed transaction.

I consider this loan a small price to pay for the deal's positive synergies and Amicus' two FDA approved therapies as described below in slides 8, 9, and 10 of BioMarin's 01/26/2026 J.P. Morgan presentation:

It is rare to find an acquisition situation that makes so much sense, at a price I regard as quite reasonable. I am aware that its first-in-disease therapy opportunity with DMX-200 is no longer first; on 04/13/2026 Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) nabbed that prize with FDA approval of its Filspari in treatment of FSGS.

The FDA approved therapies with blockbuster potential that drove the deal and made it so attractive are as shown in slides 9 and 10 below:

My 09/2025 article "Amicus Therapeutics: Turning The Corner To Profitability With 2 Developing Therapies" describes Galafold's investment merits in detail along with expectations that it reach $1 billion in revenues by 2029.

Its other FDA approved therapy expected to generate blockbuster revenues is described below in slide 10:

With its Pombiliti + Opfolda combination therapy pegged as a potential blockbuster I consider its <$5 billion dollar deal price tag as a bargain.

Viewing the pros and cons I rate BioMarin as a Buy.

There can be little doubt that BioMarin has lots of moving parts. Weighing these together I consider it as a Buy. The pieces I consider as most significant pro and con are:

Issues supporting a Buy thesis for BioMarin:

  1. Potential for ongoing VOXZOGO growth with ancillary pipeline assets to a $3+ billion revenue opportunity as discussed above;
  2. Strong supporting enzyme therapy franchise that is generating significant ongoing growing revenues;
  3. Amicus acquisition which opens two major revenue streams from approved therapies which could support a deal value of ~$8 billion as discussed below.

If you apply a 5X multiple to Galafold's 2029 peak sales it covers the entire deal price. This leaves its Pompe disease combo therapy and its yet to be approved FSGS therapy as bonus assets.

If we take a conservative value for these two of nil for FSGS and 3X peak sales for its combo therapy, it boosts the deal value to ~$8 billion.

Issues undercutting a Buy thesis:

  1. potential YUVIWEL competitive disruption of VOXZOGO revenue growth;
  2. with exception of Amicus FSGS candidate and ancillary VOXZOGO pipeline candidates, BioMarin's pipeline is weak;
  3. despite its synergies there are inevitable integration and execution risks associated with its Amicus deal.

Expectations for upcoming Q1/2026 are muted.

BioMarin's Q1/2026 earnings are just around the corner slated for release on 04/24/2026. Three key upcoming issues are sure to be discussed. These include:

  1. sNDA filing for VOXZOGO full approval as discussed above;
  2. the impact of new competition on VOXZOGO's performance;
  3. status of upcoming Q2/2026 Amicus acquisition.

With a catalyst heavy Q2 on tap expectations for Q1 are strongly muted as shown below:

Conclusion

Based on the foregoing I rate BioMarin as a Buy.

data.title: Bitmine Immersion: Unlocking Staking Rewards
data.description:
Bitmine Immersion Technologies, Inc. (BMNR) is full speed ahead developing a business beyond holding crypto assets, yet the stock trades at yearly lows. The Ethereum treasury will naturally trade mostly based on the underlying crypto prices, but the company actually has an unfolding business model. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish on the stock and the upside potential of ETH.

Staking Windfall

Beyond a crypto treasury concept, Bitmine Immersion has been pushing towards a legitimate company building out the Ethereum ecosystem. At the end of March, Bitmine Immersion announced the launch of the MAVAN (Made in America VAlidator Network) platform to offer institutions access to the largest Ethereum staking platform in the world.

The company listed the following assets in the market update on April 13:

  • Ethereum (ETH-USD) - 4,874,858 tokens.
  • Bitcoin (BTC-USD) - 198 tokens.
  • Eightco (ORBS) - 13.7 million units.
  • Beast Industries - $200 million.
  • Cash - $719 million.

Bitmine Immersion had total holdings of ~$11.8 billion with ETH at $2,206. ETH is trading up another $100+ to $2,338, now pushing the total holdings towards ~$12.4 billion, while the listed market cap is only $10.0 billion, though the company does equity raises not always captured by listed market caps.

Most crypto treasuries were built to benefit from the gains in the value of the underlying crypto assets; in this case, the focus is Ethereum. Chairman Tom Lee has also directed the company to make other investments, like the Mr. Beast deal.

The key here is that Bitmine Immersion expects to start generating over $300 million in annual revenues from ETH staking rewards based on a 2.8% yield. The company owns a 4% position in ETH and currently has 3.3 million ETH staked, or 69% of total holdings, to start the week.

Staking rewards occur due to an entity locking up a cryptocurrency in a blockchain network or staking platform to support network operations, such as validating transactions. The participants earn an award for the work via an APY; in this case, Bitmine is earning an APY of just below 2.8%.

In the weekly market update for April 6, Chairman Tom Lee again reinforced the opportunity with ETH as follows:

Ethereum continues to benefit from the dual tailwinds of Wall Street tokenizing on the blockchain and from agentic AI systems increasingly needing public and neutral blockchains.

As Bitmine Immersion continues collecting staking rewards, institutions are likely to find ETH even more attractive. The opportunity to collect a yield plus participate in the upside of the digital currency could push ETH much higher on the next market rally.

Buyback Plans

The company announced an updated share buyback from $1 billion to $4 billion. Bitmine Immersion only has a cash balance of $719 million and would potentially only find the Bitcoin assets worthwhile to sell in order to complete a share buyback, so the company doesn't actually have the capital to complete this share buyback.

Over time, Bitmine Immersion will, in theory, utilize the staking rewards to purchase assets, whether corporate shares via a share buyback or more ETH, or just collect ETH. The big question is exactly what the level of expenses will be to build the MAVAN platform and run the operation going forward.

For the quarter ended November 30, Bitmine Immersion reported quarterly expenses of around just $1 to 2 million. The company expects staking rewards revenues to reach a $75 million quarterly level soon, and any additional revenues from institutional customers are mostly unknown. Plus, the plan is to ultimately provide validation services to other blockchain networks.

The stock has a market cap at least in the $10 billion range, so the staking rewards aren't going to move the needle. Bitmine Immersion is still valued based on the ETH price, and a lot of the ultimate stock gains will depend on whether Chairman Tom Lee is correct that digital currencies will rise and that ETH will close the gap with Bitcoin due to the "payment rails" of the smaller crypto. Tom Lee has a prediction for ETH to range from $12K to $62.5K.

For now, the company is very focused on reporting quarterly results. Bitmine released the FQ2'26 10-Q without issuing a press release.

Since the results are only through the end of February, Bitmine Immersion only reported total revenues of $11 million, with $10 million from staking rewards. The company had another $5 million in interest income, but the key focus is $75 million in G&A operating expenses due to custodial fees, consulting services, and stock-based compensation.

Going forward, Bitmine will start reporting $75+ million in quarterly staking revenues. If the company can outline additional revenues from 3rd-party validation services and show a path to consistent operating income, Bitmine could shift to trading at a solid premium to the digital assets.

Unfortunately, the company isn't providing any details on the financial picture of the company outside of outlining staking revenues will reach $310 million based on current yields. Bitmine will primarily trade on ETH value if the staking revenue doesn't lead to operating income for the business to reinvest.

Naturally, the big risk is lower ETH prices. Bitmine has recently taken advantage of lower ETH prices to load up on more digital assets, but the move backfires if ETH and BTC heads lower.

The company doesn't borrow to buy digital assets, so liquidity issues aren't a risk. Bitmine has already bought 4% of the ETH token supply, so the fact this buying pressure hasn't pushed the price higher suggests major holders were using the company as exit liquidity.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Bitmine Immersion is just now fully unleashing the business model to turn the digital treasury into a DeFi business with a staking validator and a future Bitmine app. The stock provides a good opportunity to invest in the building of the ETH ecosystem and participate in additional upside versus solely owning Ethereum.
